failure_modes.text: Overweighting the base case can hide severe downside outcomes that matter more than the average scenario.
failure_modes.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?
failure_modes.mitigation: Simulate outcomes one or two standard deviations from the mean; ask whether the worst case is survivable, not just whether the base case is attractive.

failure_modes.text: Reference-class errors can make a decision look safer or riskier because the current case was matched to the wrong historical structure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Scrutinize reference-class fit: line up the assumptions that make the historical comparison valid and test each against the current case.

failure_modes.text: Sensitivity-blind analysis can create false confidence by hiding how much the conclusion changes when key inputs move.
failure_modes.mitigation: Run sensitivity analysis on every key input; if a small change in one assumption flips the conclusion, the analysis has earned less confidence than it claims.

premortem_questions.text: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What You Have to Believe Analysis

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.text: Which variable, probability, or payoff would have to move for this decision to stop being attractive?
premortem_questions.source_quote: performing a sensitivity analysis helps to test the stability of the conclusion

heuristics.text: Use decision trees or cost-benefit analysis when a choice depends on explicit branches, tradeoffs, and probabilities.
Run scenario analysis or simulation before a large commitment so the team can see the spread of plausible outcomes, not just the central case.
Treat the choice as a bet and evaluate the process quality separately from whether luck later produces a good or bad outcome.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the intervention layer on explicit bet sizing, sensitivity checks, and downside-aware branch comparison.
donor_drops: Did not preserve generic risk-management sprawl because the raw source is strongest on decisions-as-bets and scenario-backed evaluation.
open_questions: The third heuristic is phrased as an action rule rather than preserving the metaphor verbatim, but the source support is direct.

allies: The source is strong on game-theory, simulations, and structured decision diagrams.
antagonists: The raw source carries familiar pressure from impulsive System 1 judgments and false certainty.
structured_tensions: No explicit structured tension block was preserved from the raw file.
